title: Pig enrichment and rooting behaviour
subtitle: Meeting the legal requirement to provide enrichment material for pigs in the UK.
excerpt: Pigs are highly motivated to root and investigate. A pig that has nothing to root in and nothing to investigate will redirect that behaviour onto pen mates, leading to tail and ear biting. UK welfare regulations require that pigs have permanent access to enrichment material. This is not optional and applies to all holdings, including small outdoor units.
sourceNotes:
DEFRA, Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Pigs (2003). https://www.gov.uk
The Welfare of Farmed Animals (England) Regulations 2007. https://www.legislation.gov.uk/uksi/2007/2078
AHDB Pork, Enrichment for pigs. https://ahdb.org.uk
BRIDP (British Pig Industry Development Programme), Enrichment materials for pigs guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: Material provided to pigs to allow natural exploratory and rooting behaviour. Under UK welfare regulations, enrichment must be investigable, manipulable, and edible (or at least chewable). Straw is the most widely accepted enrichment material. Chains, rubber toys, and logs are permitted but are less effective than manipulable organic material.

An instinctive behaviour in which pigs use their snout to push and dig into the substrate to find food. Pigs are highly motivated to root regardless of whether they are hungry. Rooting is a natural comfort behaviour and its absence in a pig with access to loose substrate indicates illness or pain.

A harmful behaviour in which pigs bite the tails of pen mates, which can escalate from minor wound to severe injury. It is a common indicator of inadequate enrichment, overcrowding, or nutritional deficiency. Once blood is drawn, the behaviour spreads through the group rapidly. Prevention through enrichment is far more effective than treatment.

content.text: The Welfare of Farmed Animals (England) Regulations 2007 require that all pigs have permanent access to

. The requirement applies to indoor and outdoor pigs alike. The material must be safe, clean, and allow the pigs to root, chew, and investigate. An empty hard-floored pen with a hanging chain is not compliant in the spirit of the regulation, though it technically satisfies the letter.
Pigs spend 50 to 60% of their waking time foraging and exploring in natural conditions.
is so strongly motivated that pigs deprived of a substrate to root in become frustrated. This frustration redirects into pen mates: first as nosing and nudging, then as
. Outdoor pigs on rooted-out paddocks have less natural substrate available than most people assume. Providing additional enrichment on well-grazed ground is still necessary.

Straw is the gold standard enrichment material for pigs. It satisfies all criteria: it is investigable, manipulable, edible, and cheap. A small bale of straw given to a group of weaners will be completely worked over within hours. Do not wait until the arc bedding needs refreshing; provide a separate pile in the paddock for enrichment. Pigs treat it differently from bedding and will work it actively rather than lying on it.

If you have the space, designate a corner of the paddock as a permanent rooting area. Fill it with a mix of soil and composted material or leave it as a rough, uneven patch. Bury root vegetables (swede, carrot, sugar beet) at different depths and let the pigs find them. This occupies a group for hours and is completely free once the habit is established.

Compost heaps, willow branches, logs and roots (untreated wood only), hessian sacks, and hay bales all provide enrichment. Hanging objects such as chains or rubber rings have some effect but rank well below manipulable organic material in research studies. Independent research rates materials in four tiers: straw and compost are in the top tier; chains and plastic toys are in the lowest.

attrs.title: Enrichment for outdoor pigs on rooted ground
attrs.body: Many keepers assume outdoor pigs are already enriched. A worn, rooted paddock with hard bare earth provides very little to root and investigate. On that kind of ground, providing extra enrichment is just as important as in an indoor system. Paddock rotation helps in the long run, but is not always possible on a small holding.

content.text: Many keepers treat aggression between pigs as a social dominance problem. Often it is primarily an enrichment problem. Before separating a group or reducing stocking, increase enrichment provision and observe for three days. A double straw ration and a buried vegetable trail will frequently break an aggression pattern that has been building for weeks. If one pig is consistently the target of attacks, separate it and address the pen environment rather than the individuals.
